definition: I2S substitutes the binary file-format magic bytes at the start of the input so the program's format-dispatch branch routes into the correct parser; without the magic the input is treated as a different format and the entire branch-under-study is unreachable.

I2S substitutes a specific named constant (4-byte FOURCC, chunk type, table tag, colorspace signature, or protocol next-header byte) into a single dispatch field, routing execution into a particular handler or switch arm within an already-recognized format.

I2S synthesizes a structural count or length field (ELF section count, font record count, loop-entry integer) so the loop guard evaluates true and the loop body executes, reaching branches gated behind a positive-count check.

I2S assembles a well-formed protocol or grammar string scaffold from individually-intercepted string comparisons (HTTP status-line and header tokens for curl, XML prolog and namespace keywords for libxml2, SQL statement keywords for sqlite3), advancing a state machine deep enough for downstream gates to fire.

I2S substitutes a specific numeric sentinel or exact-integer constant (UINT64_MAX/kUnknownSize, checksum value, 16-bit address/RLOC identifier, small enum integer, float threshold, flag byte, channel count) that a non-dispatch comparison demands and which naive cannot reconstruct from the distance gradient alone.

I2S substitutes a single-byte ASCII character or delimiter (colon, slash, question-mark, NUL, tab, newline, bracket, dash, high-bit lead byte) at a specific text-parser cursor position, satisfying a per-character equality gate in a byte-at-a-time grammar or character-class scan.
